examples.title: Algorithm: IoT Device Class Hierarchy Design
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating class design for IoT devices with inheritance
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IoT Class Hierarchy
INPUT: None (design pattern)
OUTPUT: Class structure for IoT devices

// Base Class Definition
CLASS Device
 ATTRIBUTES
 device_id: string
 status: string
 location: string
 
 METHOD initialize(id, loc)
 device_id ← id
 location ← loc
 status ← "offline"
 
 METHOD connect()
 status ← "online"
 RETURN true
 
 METHOD disconnect()
 status ← "offline"
 RETURN true
 
 METHOD read_data()
 // Abstract method to be overridden
 RETURN null

// Derived Class: Temperature Sensor
CLASS TemperatureSensor INHERITS Device
 ATTRIBUTES
 temperature: float
 unit: string
 threshold: float
 
 METHOD initialize(id, loc, unit_celsius)
 CALL SUPER.initialize(id, loc)
 temperature ← 0.0
 unit ← unit_celsius
 threshold ← 30.0
 
 METHOD read_data()
 temperature ← GET_SENSOR_READING()
 RETURN temperature
 
 METHOD check_threshold()
 IF temperature > threshold THEN
 RETURN true
 RETURN false

// Derived Class: Smart Actuator
CLASS SmartActuator INHERITS Device
 ATTRIBUTES
 state: boolean
 power_level: integer
 
 METHOD initialize(id, loc)
 CALL SUPER.initialize(id, loc)
 state ← false
 power_level ← 0
 
 METHOD activate()
 state ← true
 power_level ← 100
 SEND_COMMAND_TO_DEVICE()
 
 METHOD deactivate()
 state ← false
 power_level ← 0
 SEND_COMMAND_TO_DEVICE()

// Main Program
BEGIN
 sensor1 ← NEW TemperatureSensor("SENS001", "Living Room", true)
 CALL sensor1.connect()
 temp ← CALL sensor1.read_data()
 
 actuator1 ← NEW SmartActuator("ACT001", "Kitchen")
 CALL actuator1.connect()
 CALL actuator1.activate()
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates object-oriented design for IoT devices. The base Device class encapsulates common attributes like device_id, status, and location with methods for connection management. TemperatureSensor inherits from Device and adds sensor-specific attributes like temperature, unit, and threshold. SmartActuator shows another specialization with state management. This hierarchy enables code reuse, polymorphism, and clean separation of concerns in IoT applications.

examples.title: Python: IoT Sensor Class with Encapsulation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of IoT sensor classes with proper encapsulation
examples.code:
"""
IoT Sensor Classes for Raspberry Pi Applications
Demonstrates class design, encapsulation, and inheritance for IoT devices
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import Optional, List
import time


class Device(ABC):
 """Base class for all IoT devices with common functionality"""
 
 def __init__(self, device_id: str, location: str):
 """Constructor to initialize device properties"""
 self._device_id = device_id # Protected attribute
 self._location = location
 self._status = "offline"
 self._last_reading_time = None
 
 @property
 def device_id(self) -> str:
 """Getter for device ID (encapsulation)"""
 return self._device_id
 
 @property
 def status(self) -> str:
 """Getter for device status"""
 return self._status
 
 @property
 def location(self) -> str:
 """Getter for device location"""
 return self._location
 
 def connect(self) -> bool:
 """Connect the device to the network"""
 self._status = "online"
 print(f"[{self._device_id}] Device connected at {self._location}")
 return True
 
 def disconnect(self) -> bool:
 """Disconnect the device from the network"""
 self._status = "offline"
 print(f"[{self._device_id}] Device disconnected")
 return True
 
 @abstractmethod
 def read_data(self):
 """Abstract method - must be implemented by subclasses"""
 pass
 
 def get_info(self) -> dict:
 """Return device information as dictionary"""
 return {
 "id": self._device_id,
 "location": self._location,
 "status": self._status,
 "type": self.__class__.__name__
 }


class TemperatureSensor(Device):
 """Temperature sensor class with threshold alerting"""
 
 def __init__(self, device_id: str, location: str, unit: str = "Celsius"):
 super().__init__(device_id, location)
 self._temperature = 0.0
 self._unit = unit
 self._threshold_high = 30.0
 self._threshold_low = 5.0
 self._reading_history: List[float] = []
 
 @property
 def temperature(self) -> float:
 """Get current temperature reading"""
 return self._temperature
 
 @property
 def unit(self) -> str:
 """Get temperature unit"""
 return self._unit
 
 def set_thresholds(self, low: float, high: float):
 """Set alert thresholds for temperature"""
 self._threshold_low = low
 self._threshold_high = high
 
 def read_data(self) -> float:
 """Read temperature from sensor (simulated)"""
 # In real IoT: read from GPIO/sensor library
 import random
 self._temperature = round(random.uniform(15.0, 35.0), 2)
 self._last_reading_time = time.time()
 self._reading_history.append(self._temperature)
 
 # Keep only last 10 readings
 if len(self._reading_history) > 10:
 self._reading_history.pop(0)
 
 return self._temperature
 
 def check_alert(self) -> Optional[str]:
 """Check if temperature exceeds thresholds"""
 if self._temperature > self._threshold_high:
 return f"HIGH TEMPERATURE ALERT: {self._temperature}°{self._unit[0]}"
 elif self._temperature < self._threshold_low:
 return f"LOW TEMPERATURE ALERT: {self._temperature}°{self._unit[0]}"
 return None
 
 def get_average(self) -> float:
 """Calculate average of recent readings"""
 if not self._reading_history:
 return 0.0
 return sum(self._reading_history) / len(self._reading_history)


class SmartActuator(Device):
 """Smart actuator class for controlling devices"""
 
 def __init__(self, device_id: str, location: str, actuator_type: str = "generic"):
 super().__init__(device_id, location)
 self._actuator_type = actuator_type
 self._is_active = False
 self._power_level = 0
 
 @property
 def is_active(self) -> bool:
 """Check if actuator is currently active"""
 return self._is_active
 
 @property
 def power_level(self) -> int:
 """Get current power level (0-100)"""
 return self._power_level
 
 def activate(self, power: int = 100) -> bool:
 """Activate the actuator with specified power level"""
 if not (0 <= power <= 100):
 raise ValueError("Power level must be between 0 and 100")
 
 self._power_level = power
 self._is_active = True
 # In real IoT: send command to GPIO pin
 print(f"[{self._device_id}] Activated at {power}% power")
 return True
 
 def deactivate(self) -> bool:
 """Deactivate the actuator"""
 self._power_level = 0
 self._is_active = False
 print(f"[{self._device_id}] Deactivated")
 return True
 
 def read_data(self) -> dict:
 """Return actuator state data"""
 return {
 "active": self._is_active,
 "power_level": self._power_level,
 "type": self._actuator_type
 }


# Main demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create sensor instance
 temp_sensor = TemperatureSensor("TEMP_001", "Living Room", "Celsius")
 temp_sensor.connect()
 temp_sensor.set_thresholds(10.0, 28.0)
 
 # Read multiple temperature values
 print("\n--- Temperature Readings ---")
 for i in range(5):
 temp = temp_sensor.read_data()
 print(f"Reading {i+1}: {temp}°C")
 alert = temp_sensor.check_alert()
 if alert:
 print(f" ALERT: {alert}")
 
 print(f"\nAverage temperature: {temp_sensor.get_average():.2f}°C")
 
 # Create actuator instance
 print("\n--- Smart Actuator ---")
 fan = SmartActuator("FAN_001", "Living Room", "cooling_fan")
 fan.connect()
 fan.activate(75)
 
 print(f"\nFan status: {fan.read_data()}")
 
 # Display device info
 print("\n--- Device Information ---")
 print(f"Sensor: {temp_sensor.get_info()}")
 print(f"Actuator: {fan.get_info()}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates core OOP concepts for IoT: (1) Abstract base class Device with ABC for defining interfaces, (2) Encapsulation using protected attributes (_prefix) and properties for controlled access, (3) Inheritance with TemperatureSensor and SmartActuator extending Device, (4) Polymorphism through abstract method read_data(), (5) Constructor (__init__) using super() for initialization. The code includes type hints, docstrings, and simulates real IoT sensor/actuator patterns.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for most operations; O(n) for get_average() where n is history size

examples.title: Java: IoT Device Class Hierarchy
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating classes, inheritance, and interfaces for IoT
examples.explanation: This Java implementation demonstrates comprehensive OOP concepts for IoT: (1) Abstract class IoTDevice as base with encapsulation via private fields and public getters, (2) Interface ReadableDevice for polymorphism, (3) Inheritance with TemperatureSensor and SmartActuator extending IoTDevice, (4) Enum for DeviceStatus representing device states, (5) Method overriding with @Override annotations, (6) Polymorphism through the ReadableDevice interface allowing mixed device lists. The code shows proper Java conventions with camelCase naming, access modifiers, and type-safe implementations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for basic operations; O(n) for getAverage() where n is history size
